(b) Run an adaptive Phase 2b/3 with interim at n=150 for futility/dose selection; total n up to 450; cost $55M; readout 30 months; requires partnering within 6 months to fund. (c) Pause UC, pivot to Crohn’s (new IND amendment), run smaller Phase 2 (n=120) focusing on biomarkers; cost $18M; readout 14 months; keeps UC option alive but delays it. Ask: Which single option (a), (b), or (c) should the CEO choose now?
problems.R0: Choose Option (a): run the U.S.-only Phase 2b (n=240, 3 arms, 16 weeks), powered for clinical remission, with baseline ALT >1.5x ULN exclusion and mandatory monthly LFTs. Rationale: (1) Survival math — cash is $38M with $4.5M/month burn (~8.4 months runway). Option (b) at $55M is unaffordable without a partner in 6 months, giving a counterparty full leverage before any de-risking readout, and (c)'s Crohn's pivot burns $18M plus 14 months to answer a different question while abandoning the asset's strongest signal. Option (a)'s $32M is the only path that fits the raise-after-clear-readout financing reality, and the $38M cushion plus staged spend keeps the company alive to a value-inflecting readout. (2) Signal quality — the Phase 2a efficacy miss (14%, p=0.08) is underpowered noise, but the -45% calprotectin effect and clean dose-response are genuinely encouraging biomarkers; UC remission (not Crohn's biomarkers) is the registration-relevant endpoint FDA will require, so (a) directly tests the thesis that matters for value. (3) Safety is manageable, not disqualifying — 3/48 reversible ALT elevations is a Hy's-law-style watch item, and (a)'s design (excluding elevated baseline ALT, monthly LFTs, dose-ranging) is exactly the risk-mitigation FDA will want and can be socialized at the reserved Type C meeting. Use the Type C meeting to lock endpoint definitions, the hepatic monitoring plan, and dose selection. Load-bearing assumptions: (i) a credible, non-dilutive-poison equity raise is achievable on a clear 2b remission readout — if capital markets are shut regardless, reconsider partnering-dependent (b); (ii) the ALT signal is idiosyncratic/reversible and not a dose-limiting Hy's-law liability that would sink the program at any dose; (iii) $32M/18-month estimates hold within ~15% and the 18-month readout precedes cash exhaustion given staged enrollment spend, which must be confirmed against the enrollment-adjusted burn curve before committing. Kill/pivot trigger: if the Type C meeting reveals FDA demands a materially larger or longer 2b (pushing cost toward (b)'s envelope) or imposes a hepatic hold, revert to seeking a partner rather than self-funding.
problems.R1:
Choose Option (a) — U.S.-only Phase 2b (n=240, 3 arms, 16 weeks) powered for remission — but ship it as a CONDITIONED, staged commitment, not an unconditional 'start now.' Concrete structure:

1) SURVIVAL FIX (mandatory, in the option definition now). Raw runway math is fatal as written: ~$4.5M/month base burn plus ~$1.8M/month trial spend exhausts $38M in ~6 months, well before an 18-month readout. So (a) is only viable if paired with: (i) immediate non-trial burn cuts to extend base runway; (ii) a built-in early value inflection — a pre-specified interim biomarker/endoscopy look (fecal calprotectin + centrally-read endoscopic response) at ~n=120/12 weeks, ~6-9 months in — that creates a financeable milestone BEFORE full remission readout; and (iii) a bridge financing plan (tranched raise, venture debt, or a non-exclusive regional/ex-US partnership) sized to reach that interim, NOT the final readout. If none of (i)-(iii) can be arranged, the correct choice flips to (b)'s partnering-dependent path or a smaller-scope trial — do not self-fund into insolvency.

2) COMMITMENT SEQUENCING FIX. The 21-day commitment is a commitment to the PATH and the Type C agenda, not to irreversible vendor/site/CMC spend. Gate all large cash outlays on the Type C outcome (60 days). Use the 21-day window only for reversible prep (protocol synopsis, budget, financing outreach). This removes the 'start now, unwind later' failure mode.

3) HEPATIC PACKAGE FIX (decision-critical). A ~6.25% rate of ALT >5x ULN is a serious, potentially program-limiting signal, not merely a 'watch item.' Drop the 'Hy's-law-style' framing (Hy's Law requires concurrent ALT/AST + bilirubin without cholestasis — not established here). Bring to Type C a full hepatic strategy: LFTs every 1-2 weeks for first 8 weeks then monthly, explicit stopping/rechallenge rules, bilirubin/ALP monitoring for Hy's Law confirmation, DILI adjudication committee, and a pre-specified exposure-response analysis. Treat 'FDA imposes hepatic hold or demands exposure-limiting monitoring incompatible with a viable oral chronic therapy' as a program KILL trigger, not a pivot-to-partner footnote.

4) POWERING FIX. Do not assume 240/3-arm is powered on the 14% (p=0.08) delta. Before Type C, run formal sample-size modeling using conservative true-effect scenarios (10-14%), realistic placebo remission rates, multiplicity for dose comparisons, and the 12->16 week endpoint shift. If honest powering requires materially more patients or a single active-dose comparison, either narrow to 2 arms (highest-signal dose vs placebo) to preserve power/budget, or accept this pushes toward (b) and re-decide. Lock endpoint definitions, steroid taper, and endoscopic/histologic components at Type C.

Net: (a) remains the best expected-value path IF and ONLY IF the interim value-inflection + bridge financing + acceptable hepatic package are secured. It is explicitly conditional; failing conditions 1 or 3 redirect to (b) or a de-scoped trial rather than a doomed self-funded 2b.

dispositions.note: Conceded as material and largely correct. Removed the claim that $38M 'staged spend' alone finances (a); added mandatory burn cuts, a pre-specified interim biomarker/endoscopy inflection to enable an earlier raise, and a bridge-financing requirement, with an explicit flip to (b) if these can't be arranged.

Conceded. Dropped the misleading 'Hy's-law-style' label, upgraded the signal from 'watch item' to potentially program-limiting, specified 1-2 week early LFTs, stopping/rechallenge rules, bilirubin/ALP, DILI adjudication, exposure-response, and made a hepatic hold a hard kill trigger gated at Type C.

Conceded. Removed the assumption that 240/3-arm is adequately powered; required formal sample-size modeling under conservative effect sizes with multiplicity, with a fallback to a 2-arm design or re-decision toward (b) if underpowered.
Conceded. Reframed the 21-day step as path/agenda commitment only, gating all major cash outlays on Type C feedback, eliminating the irreversible 'start-then-unwind' risk.

problems.problem:
You are the lead underwriter for a regional carrier and must decide whether—and how—to quote a 12‑month property + GL package for a mid-market metal fabrication plant in Ohio. Effective date is 21 days away; reinsurance treaty requires bind decisions ≥10 days before inception. The insured reports $18M annual revenue, 95 employees, and a replacement cost estimate of $12.5M for a 120,000 sq ft facility built in 1984 with a partial roof replacement in 2016. They store up to 60,000 gallons of cutting oils and solvents (Class IIIB/II), have an on-site paint booth, and run two shifts. Loss history: one $240k fire loss 3 years ago (electrical), two slip-and-fall GL claims totaling $85k over 5 years. Current carrier is non-renewing due to “hazard appetite shift.” Engineering inspection can’t occur for 45 days.

You must choose ONE option:
(a) Offer $10M property limit with $100k wind/hail deductible, $50k AOP deductible, $3.2M premium, subject to immediate installation of monitored sprinkler supervisory alarms within 30 days.
(b) Offer full $12.5M property limit with $250k AOP deductible, exclude paint booth fire, $2.6M premium, no immediate risk improvements.
(c) Decline to quote until inspection results are available, risking the account but avoiding blind exposure.

Ask: Which option (a), (b), or (c) do you choose?
problems.R0: Choose (a): Quote the $10M property limit with $100k wind/hail and $50k AOP deductibles at $3.2M premium, conditioned on installation of monitored sprinkler supervisory alarms within 30 days. RATIONALE: This is the only option that binds the account within treaty deadlines (10-day cutoff) while managing the dominant peril—fire in an aging (1984) fab plant with a paint booth, 60k gallons of combustible/flammable oils and solvents, and a prior $240k electrical fire. Option (b) is disqualifying: excluding paint booth fire while writing full limit at a LOWER premium ($2.6M) inverts the risk—paint booths and solvent storage are the exact high-severity fire exposure driving the incumbent's non-renewal, and pricing this hazard-heavy account below the more protective option makes no actuarial sense. Option (c) forfeits a $3.2M premium account and cannot recover it—the 45-day inspection horizon runs past both the treaty bind deadline and effective date, so 'wait for inspection' effectively means decline. Option (a) prices to hazard, uses deductible structure to retain frequency (slip/fall GL) and shock losses on the insured, sub-limits total exposure at $10M vs. a $12.5M RC (an ~80% margin the insured can absorb or arrange DIC), and the 30-day sprinkler supervisory alarm warranty is a concrete, verifiable risk-improvement that partially substitutes for the delayed engineering visit. STRUCTURE THE BIND: (1) make the alarm installation a condition-precedent/warranty with cancellation right if not certified within 30 days; (2) add a mandatory post-bind engineering inspection at 45 days with a re-underwriting/mid-term amendment or cancellation trigger if findings materially worsen the fire exposure; (3) apply a protective safeguards/flammable-storage warranty and confirm the paint booth is UL-listed with functioning suppression before or shortly after inception; (4) obtain confirmation the $240k fire's electrical root cause was remediated. LOAD-BEARING ASSUMPTIONS: (a) treaty capacity is available at the $10M layer and pricing clears the ceded cost of hazard class; (b) the insured can and will complete monitored alarm installation in 30 days and permit inspection at 45 days; (c) the incumbent's non-renewal is genuinely appetite-driven, not a concealed adverse-loss signal—if broker/loss-run diligence reveals undisclosed fire or code-compliance issues, revert toward decline; (d) $12.5M RC is credible and not understated for a 120k sq ft facility, otherwise re-scope limit adequacy before bind.
problems.R1:
Choose (a), but REPRICED AND RESTRUCTURED to fix the coinsurance, warranty-enforceability, and controls gaps. Bind the account within treaty deadlines while making the fire-severity protections actually load-bearing.

DECISION: Quote a $12.5M limit written on AGREED VALUE / REPLACEMENT COST with the COINSURANCE CLAUSE SUSPENDED (agreed-value endorsement) so there is no underinsurance penalty. Retain the aggressive deductibles ($100k wind/hail, $50k AOP) and reprice to ~$3.2M to reflect the full limit and hazard class (do NOT write $10M below RC and rely on a hypothetical DIC that does not exist at bind). If treaty capacity or line size forces a sub-$12.5M layer, then include an explicit MARGIN CLAUSE / no-coinsurance (blanket agreed value) so any layer written cannot trigger a coinsurance penalty. Never write a bare 80% ITV on a high-severity fire risk.

WHY NOT (b)/(c): (b) still inverts risk—excluding paint booth fire at a lower premium removes the exact severity driver; reject. (c) equals decline given the 45-day inspection horizon runs past both treaty cutoff and inception; reject.

RISK CONTROL — REPLACE SUPERVISORY-ONLY WITH ENFORCEABLE, SEVERITY-RELEVANT TERMS:
(1) Attach a properly drafted PROTECTIVE SAFEGUARDS ENDORSEMENT (Ohio-compliant) specifying each safeguard (sprinkler system in service, paint booth suppression/interlocks operational, flammable-liquid storage controls, hot-work permit program, monitored central-station alarms) with clear notice-of-impairment and cure language. Recognize that in Ohio a post-loss denial for PSE breach may require a causal connection and clean drafting—so DO NOT rely on the warranty as the primary risk substitute. Instead:
(2) ACCELERATE the engineering inspection: order an expedited/desktop pre-bind survey or third-party virtual inspection of the sprinkler design (ESFR vs CMDA, density, water supply), in-rack/foam-water protection and containment for the 60k gallons of Class II/IIIB liquids, paint booth UL-listing and suppression interlocks, electrical remediation of the prior fire cause, and housekeeping/hot-work controls. If a pre-bind virtual survey is not feasible, condition bind on receipt of the insured's current NFPA/AHJ sprinkler certificates, fire marshal reports, and paint booth compliance documentation BEFORE bind.
(3) Make the full physical engineering inspection at ≤45 days a MANDATORY post-bind condition with a re-underwriting/mid-term amendment or cancellation trigger (per Ohio cancellation rules and notice periods) if design/water-supply/containment are found inadequate; price in the cost of required improvements as subjectivities with defined completion dates.
(4) Apply a flammable-liquid storage and hot-work warranty; confirm the $240k electrical fire root cause was remediated as a documented subjectivity.

LOAD-BEARING CHECKS BEFORE BIND: treaty capacity available at full $12.5M agreed-value limit and pricing clears ceded hazard cost; documentary evidence (certs/fire marshal/loss runs) confirms non-renewal is appetite-driven, not concealed adverse loss; sprinkler system is confirmed appropriate for the occupancy or improvements are scheduled as subjectivities. If pre-bind documentation reveals inadequate sprinkler design or undisclosed loss/code issues that cannot be cured within terms, revert to decline (c).

dispositions.note: Changed from $10M/80% ITV to full $12.5M limit on agreed-value/RC with coinsurance suspended; if a sub-limit layer is forced, require a margin clause / no-coinsurance so no underinsurance penalty can apply. Removed reliance on a non-existent DIC assumption.

Replaced supervisory-alarm-as-core-substitute with a properly drafted Ohio-compliant PSE (notice/cure/causal-connection aware) and explicitly demoted the warranty from primary risk control; primary control is now expedited inspection/documentation before bind.

Added expedited pre-bind virtual/desktop survey plus required current NFPA/AHJ/fire marshal and paint booth certs addressing sprinkler design/density, water supply, foam-water/containment for flammable liquids, booth interlocks, electrical remediation, and hot-work/housekeeping—so terms actually contemplate the real severity drivers.
